name: Linear Relationships
description: When one quantity changes at a constant rate with another (straight line)

content.overview: A linear relationship is when two quantities are connected in the simplest way: one is a constant multiple of the other plus a constant. Graph = straight line. {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
**The equation:** y = mx + b

- m = slope (steepness)
- b = y-intercept (where line crosses y-axis)
- x = input
- y = output

**Examples in physics:** {{viz:chart-01}}
- Distance = speed × time (if constant speed): d = st (straight line through origin)
- Temperature (Celsius) vs Temperature (Kelvin): K = C + 273.15 (straight line)
- Force = mass × acceleration: F = ma (straight line through origin)
- Cost = number of items × price (straight line)

**How to recognize linear:**
- Graph is a straight line
- When x increases by 1, y always increases by the same amount
- The change is consistent

**Why linear relationships are important:** {{viz:anim-01}}
- Easy to understand
- Easy to calculate
- Very common in physics
- If you know m and b, you know everything about the relationship

When you see "y = mx + b" in a physics problem, you know it's a straight line and you can solve it easily.
keyPoints: Graph is always a straight line
content.whyItMatters: Many physics laws are linear or approximately linear. Recognizing them saves time in problem-solving.
